title: Alloxylon wickhamii
taxonomy: Alloxylon wickhamii is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Proteaceae. It belongs to the genus Alloxylon, which is a group of tropical trees native to the Pacific Islands. The plant is native to the island of New Caledonia. Alloxylon wickhamii is also known as Wickham's wattle or Wickham's stringybark. It is a rare and endangered species due to habitat loss and other environmental factors.

However, there is limited scientific research on its uses, and more studies are needed to confirm its efficacy. intro: Alloxylon wickhamii, also known as the mountain tamarind or Wau tamarind, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rutaceae, native to Papua New Guinea and the island of New Guinea. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can help to alleviate digestive issues, fever, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Alloxylon wickhamii include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Alloxylon wickhamii is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used in the form of a poultice or topical cream to treat skin conditions and wounds.
